data.title: Preformed Line Products Company's Epic Run Is Almost Over
data.description:
Nobody has a perfect track record when it comes to timing in the market. But it is always nice to get a call exactly right. A great example of this can be seen by looking at Preformed Line Products Company (PLPC), a company that produces products dedicated to the power conductor and fiber communication cable spaces. It also produces protective closures that are used for fixed-line communication networks, as well as other miscellaneous related offerings.

Before September of last year, I had taken a more neutral stance on the business. This was based on evaluation. However, back in September of last year, I decided to upgrade it to a soft "Buy" because of how rapid growth was. Since then, the stock has jumped 38.6%, comfortably outperforming the 2.7% rise of the S&P 500 over the same window of time. This can be attributed to continued rapid growth on both the top and bottom lines.

To be honest with you, the stock is reaching a point where a downgrade is sensible. Again, this is a great company that's doing a fantastic job of expanding. At some point, however, the stock becomes fairly valued. Having said that, in light of the rapid growth that the company has exhibited, I will say that I am not ready to pull the trigger on an upgrade just yet. I am getting perilously close. But I would argue that another 10% to 20% increase is not unreasonable to expect in light of the overall picture of the business.

Nearing a downgrade

Based out of my home state of Ohio, Preformed Line Products Company is a fascinating business. As I mentioned already, the company provides certain industrial goods. Specifically, I am talking about those dedicated to the construction and maintenance of networks for the energy, telecommunication, cable operator, data communication, and other similar industries. Mostly, the company supports, protects, connects, terminates, and secures wires and cables. This is a rather simple business. But management has achieved, over its lifetime, significant expansion, with sales and manufacturing operations spread across 20 different nations. The business is, of course, mostly focused on the U.S. market. As of the end of the most recent quarter, about 45.4% of sales came from here. However, it does generate revenue from other parts of the Americas, the Asia Pacific region, and the EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) parts of the world.

This may not seem like all that exciting a space from a growth perspective. But management has achieved remarkable results in recent years. In the third quarter of 2025, revenue amounted to $178.1 million. That's 21.2% above the $147 million the company reported a year earlier. Although net profits contracted from $7.7 million to $2.6 million, on an adjusted basis, we saw an increase from $7.7 million to $10.3 million. Operating cash flow shot up from $9.4 million to $18.9 million. And if we strip out changes in working capital, we get a near doubling from $13 million to $25.5 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $15.7 million to $18.8 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. This demonstrates that the expansion experienced in the third quarter was not a blip on the radar. Rather, the company is consistently growing, particularly from a revenue and cash flow perspective. But to understand the business better, we need to dig a bit deeper.

The biggest growth engine for the company recently has been its PLP-USA segment. This involves the U.S. operations for the company and serves as the backbone of the business. In the third quarter of 2025, revenue was $80.8 million. That's 23.2% above the $65.6 million reported a year earlier. According to management, this jump was driven by higher volumes associated with energy and communications and markets. To be honest with you, management is frustratingly opaque. They do provide more detail whenever they file an annual report. But beyond that, they are quite silent beyond what I covered already. But what we do know, however, is that there are certain catalysts that have been driving expansion here.

In a previous article about the company, I talked about $42.45 billion under the BEAD (Broadband Equity, Access, and Deployment) Program. This was an initiative signed into law back in 2021 under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. This objective is aimed at expanding high-speed Internet access to unserved and underserved parts of the United States. The entire goal of this is to improve access to high-quality Internet and telecommunications services in rural and low-income parts of the United States. It applies not only to the states but also to U.S. territories. And most of the funding involves the deployment of fiber optic broadband infrastructure, though some can be allocated to certain digital skills programs, broadband affordability initiatives, and the like.

On Feb. 9 of this year, the National Telecommunications and Information Administration, or NTIA, which oversees this funding, announced that 50 BEAD final proposals had been approved. This was one of the 56 that states and territories had submitted. Total funding from these investments is estimated, according to the organization, to capture about $21 billion of savings for the American people. And hopefully, with the organization pushing to approve the remaining proposals, that number will increase further. You also have on top of this continued investments in data centers, as well as companies making their own organic investments in fiber rollout, and it's not difficult to imagine why revenue for this segment grew so much.

This was not the only area of growth for the business. In fact, its Americas segment saw even more rapid expansion year over year. Revenue shot up 47.8% from $19.9 million to $29.3 million. This surge in revenue was driven by higher volumes and energy product sales and greater demand for communications offerings throughout the region. It is worth noting that a good jump in the demand for the business came from its acquisition of JAP Telecom back in May of 2025. This was a rather small purchase for the business, costing shareholders only $5.8 million. But as a leading designer, producer, and supplier of connectivity solutions for the South American telecommunications infrastructure market, with operations centered in Brazil, this is an interesting transaction aimed at boosting international exposure.

By comparison, the other segments for the company grew at a slower pace. The EMEA region, for instance, saw revenue grow 15.6%, from $32.9 million to $38.1 million year over year. Special industry sales and a growth in energy product sales helped the business. And then finally, the Asia Pacific region saw revenue grow 4.3% from $28.6 million to $29.9 million. This tepid growth was attributable to higher volumes in specialty industry sales also.

Overall, I would say that the picture here is quite positive. Management unfortunately does not really provide much in the way of detail for shareholders. But based on how the company performed during the first nine months of 2025, it was on track to generate adjusted net profits of $48.3 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $102.1 million, and EBITDA of $88.8 million. Powered with these estimates, as well as the results that the company saw the previous fiscal year, we can see in the chart below how the stock is priced at the moment.

Honestly, these multiples fit in the fair value range as I typically define it. However, we should not discount the rapid growth the company is experiencing. And with new projects here in the US approved and additional investments certain to be made into the industries that are most important to it, I am more flexible on valuation than I normally would be. In the aforementioned chart above, you can also see another reason for my optimism. This shows how the stock is priced compared to companies that have some similarities to it. It is, across the board, the cheapest of the prospects. And in the chart below, you can see what would happen if the stock were to experience the same trading multiples as the cheapest of the five comparable firms, as well as if it were to trade at the average of the multiples of the five businesses. This is admittedly a significant range to work with. But in all but one case, we are looking at double-digit or triple-digit upside. This is encouraging, to say the least.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, it is my belief that the market has appropriately rewarded shareholders for the rapid growth that Preformed Line Products Company has been exhibiting. Normally at this point, I would take a more neutral stance on the business. But considering how attractive growth prospects are likely to be, and considering the relative valuation of the company when stacked up against other similar firms, I believe that maintaining it as a soft "Buy" makes the most sense for now.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: No Rate Changes In Near Future
data.description:
"Federal Reserve governor Stephen Miran dialed back his calls for how deeply the Fed should cut rates this year, telling an interviewer that recent data have reflected a stronger economy than he had expected."

This comes from the February 20 edition of the Wall Street Journal.

Miran admits that “The labor market came in a little bit better than I came to expect over the last few months.”

The WSJ article goes on: "Miran’s new position, which implies a full percentage point of rate cuts this year from the current level of 3.5% to 3.75%, still leaves him among the Fed’s most dovish officials, contrasting sharply with the median official’s projection that the Fed should cut rates by just a quarter point this year."

"Yet the interview is notable because it suggests more distance between Miran and the economic-policy views of the White House."

And other members of the Fed's Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) have also, in the past week, expressed doubts about the need for too much rate cutting this year.

This economic situation is combined with the fact that the FOMC moved the focus of its securities portfolio reduction from "quantitative tightening" to "quantitative easing" around the first of December.

The Fed added another $8.0 billion in securities held outright to its portfolio in the past banking week, bringing the total increase in the securities portfolio to $84.5 billion since the move to quantitative easing.

Since the change in focus, the Fed has done very little else to alter its balance sheet position.

The increase in the size of the securities portfolio since November 26, 2025, currently looks like this.

The "excess reserves" in the commercial banking system have remained relatively constant since the Fed shifted to the current quantitative easing stance.

The fact that these balances are remaining relatively constant supports the feeling that the Fed is not really looking to move the policy rate of interest in the near future.

That is, it seems as if the market pressures for a reduction in the Fed's policy rate of interest are not there. This is leading members of the FOMC to talk about fewer rate moves this year and with little pressure to lower it further at the present time.

M2 money supply growth seems to be keeping to a relatively steady pace.

The M2 money stock has grown by 4.9 percent in the last year, which seems to be about where the Federal Reserve would like it to be.

The banking system is still filled with plenty of liquidity from the earlier efforts of the Fed to protect the commercial banks, but the behavior of the banks in response to the Fed's efforts at quantitative tightening seems to indicate that the banking system is working with the Federal Reserve to move into a time when the system is more reasonably functioning.

Moving into the next stage, the return to quantitative easing, is being implemented because the banking system is keeping itself under control.

But this will be one of the big issues going forward.

If the banking system sees the "government" moving into a time of less discipline as Chairman Powell departs from his leadership position and Mr. Warsh does not establish a drive to maintain control of the banking system, things could get "out of control."

I have just written a post discussing the fact that Mr. Powell is "Leaving with Things Much Better Than Anyone Could Have Expected."

It is going to be up to Kevin Warsh to take what is being given to him and move things on to a "better spot," one that results in a very active economy, but one without a scenario of more rapid inflation dominating the scene.

I think more and more people are seeing that some of the more drastic moves threatened by the Trump administration are totally unnecessary to get what the president wants.

The most important fact in this situation is that Powell's leadership has brought the U.S. economy to where it is and that the need to "push" to a policy of expansion really doesn't exist anymore.

The Federal Reserve is exerting quantitative easing.

The problem that could trip us up here is that Mr. Warsh may not like the quantitative tightening-quantitative easing duo.

It has been reported that Warsh did not like this policy approach...and that is one reason that he left the Federal Reserve before his term was over.

So, Mr. Warsh may want to change how the Fed does policy.

Does he prefer to control interest rates and move them around as the primary policy tool...get away from a quantitative approach to the conduct of monetary policy?

Personally, I hope not.

We will start to learn more about where Warsh stands when he gets reviewed by Congress.

Right now, though, the picture looks like...quantitative easing going forward with few, if any, reductions in the Fed's policy rate of interest in the near future.

data.title: Q4 2025 GDP And December PCE: Economic Growth Slows, Inflation Doesn't
data.description:
This morning’s releases presented a conflicting macro narrative, pairing weak headline growth with renewed firmness in inflation. The Q4 GDP report looked soft at first glance, but much of the downside was driven by temporary government shutdown effects, leaving underlying private demand closer to the pace seen through most of 2025. At the same time, the December personal income and PCE data pointed to consumers continuing to spend despite slower income growth and showed a clear pickup in price pressures. Together, the data shift the focus away from the growth scare implied by the headline GDP figure and back toward inflation dynamics and the policy path they imply for the months ahead.

Q4 2025 GDP

The advance estimate of US GDP growth in the final quarter of 2025 came in very soft, with the government shutdown affecting the headline economic activity indicator more than forecasters and models expected. Q4 2025 real GDP growth was estimated at 1.4% in the release this morning, well below the consensus forecast of 3.0%, below the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow nowcast estimate of 3.0% (which was as high as 5% at one point), and even below the New York Fed’s more conservative nowcast of 2.71%. What exactly happened here? Let’s dive into the details.

In the broadest view of the details, we can see that the government shutdown was a major drag on the positive contributions to growth from consumer spending and private investment. And at the fringe, net exports made a marginal positive contribution.

  • Consumer spending increased at a SAAR of 2.4% in Q4 2025, below the growth rates seen in the last two quarters. Spending on goods was actually slightly down (-0.1%) due to a decline in nondurable goods spending (-0.9%), but this was offset by strong growth in spending on services of 3.4%, roughly in line with service sector expenditures in Q3. Within services spending, health care spending increased the most (+$40.6 billion) and was the largest contributor to GDP growth within the consumer spending component.
  • Gross private domestic investment saw its strongest growth since Q1, increasing at a SAAR of 3.8%. Within the segment, we see the trend of weakness in real estate preserved, as residential investment fell at a SAAR of -1.5% (fourth consecutive quarterly decline) and nonresidential investment rose at a SAAR of 3.7%. Nonresidential investment growth looks to have been driven by AI once again, as the top contributions to growth came from information processing equipment (+0.65 ppts) and intellectual property products R&D (+0.25 ppts).

  • The net export component was watched closely in the Atlanta Fed nowcast due to its volatile monthly swings (see table above), but it ended up being largely inconsequential to quarterly growth in Q4, adding just 8 bps to headline real GDP growth. Both exports and imports declined in the quarter, at SAARs of -0.9% and -1.3%, respectively. The slight positive contribution is a result of the drop in imports offsetting the drop in exports.
  • The component that was the major surprise this quarter was government spending and investment, declining at a SAAR of -5.1%. The surprise wasn’t in the direction of the growth but in the magnitude. The BEA specifically estimated that federal government economic activity contracted at a SAAR of -16.6%, the 2nd largest contraction in almost 15 years (the largest in that period is Q3 2020). The size of the decline was affected by both the government shutdown from October 1st to November 2nd and the large decline in the government workforce due to contract buyouts. The BEA could not fully quantify the effects of the former but estimated that the latter “subtracted about 1.0 ppts from real GDP growth in Q4.”

What exactly do we make of this GDP report that was clearly distorted by the impacts of the government shutdown (a temporary event where the effects are typically reversed in the next quarter)? Well, if we exclude that segment, looking at final sales to private domestic purchasers, we see that growth was at a more stable pace of 2.4%, slightly lower than the 2.9% readings of that indicator in Q2 and Q3. Of course, this slowdown in private sector activity could have also seen some residual indirect effects from the shutdown, like federal workers not being able to spend as much due to disruptions in their wages.

Outside of the government shutdown, the Q4 report has similar features to other quarters in 2025. Health care spending once again was a major contributor to growth, similar to how the industry is carrying employment growth, and AI spending is clearly making up for weaknesses elsewhere, like real estate and nonresidential structures. Removing these two specific areas, and growth is around 0%. Nevertheless, growth is growth, and even if it’s concentrated in certain areas of the economy, it is still happening.

December Personal Income & Outlays

In addition to Q4 2025 GDP data, the BEA released the final monthly personal income and outlays report of 2025, featuring data for December. It showed that the slightly more subdued gains in income and spending in Q4 vs. Q3 continued into the final month of the quarter. Personal income and disposable income both increased 0.3% MoM in December, and both were slightly below personal spending growth of 0.4% MoM. Unlike the GDP report, these numbers were all in line with the consensus estimates.

The details of the personal income growth data in December show an extra boost from personal transfer receipts. Employee compensation increased 0.2% MoM, the weakest since June 2026, and accounted for around 36% of income growth, while transfer receipts accounted for about 45% of income growth. The BEA notes that this temporary boost from transfer receipts was due to “a settlement paid by a domestic electric utility to U.S. households to compensate for claims of personal injury, property damage, and other losses related to the 2023 Maui wildfire.”

Turning to trends in spending, we see a similar, troubling trend. For the eighth month in a row, the growth in personal outlays outpaced the growth in personal incomes, meaning that consumers had to dip into savings once again to fuel the growth in spending. Similar to the Q4 GDP data, the growth in spending on services (+0.7% MoM) more than offset a decline in goods spending (-0.1% MoM). Consumers once again increased their spending the most on housing & utilities, recreation services, and health care, and these three areas accounted for 78% of the spending growth in December. The result of this rise in consumption was the personal savings rate dropping to 3.6%, the lowest since 2022.

PCE Inflation

Consumption didn’t actually increase much in volume terms in December; real PCE growth was actually 0.1% MoM. Instead, consumer prices increased at a relatively firm rate during the month. The PCE price index grew 0.4% MoM, ahead of expectations of a 0.3% MoM print and the fastest since February 2025. The core PCE index was up 0.4% MoM, also ahead of expectations of 0.3% MoM and the fastest since February 2025. The hot monthly prints pushed the annual PCE and core PCE inflation rates up to 2.9% YoY and 3.0% YoY, respectively. The latter print leaves the Fed’s preferred inflation rate at the highest it has been since April 2024, suggesting a turn against the disinflationary trend.

Interestingly, it wasn’t service prices that increased the most in December. Instead, the hottest price growth came from durable goods (+0.5% MoM) and food (+0.4% MoM), both contributing to a strong +0.4% MoM increase in the broader PCE goods price index. The PCE services index still increased at a decent rate, +0.3% MoM, but this isn’t too far off from what we have seen services prices do in 2025. With goods spending in December and Q4 as a whole decreasing, one has to wonder if the increase in goods prices is more tariff-related. On an annual basis, goods prices are up 1.7% YoY and durable goods prices are up 2.1% YoY; these are the highest YoY gains in 3-4 years.

Market Reaction

The market reaction to both reports appears to be focused on the hot inflation prints in the monthly PCE report. This is likely because the data in the GDP report are largely impacted by temporary factors, like the government shutdown, and the details were a bit mixed. Typically, one would expect a downside surprise in growth to be translated into more dovish trading, but markets clearly focused on the upside surprise in the PCE price indexes, which translated to a more hawkish bias.

We see in the charts above that the release of both reports at 8:30 EST pushed S&P futures lower, brought Treasury yields higher, and caused the USD to be a bit firmer. These trends held for most of the morning and into the opening of the US stock market until they broke down in response to the bigger news of the US Supreme Court ruling against Trump’s use of the IEEPA to impose tariffs, released around 10 am EST. As a result, we see stocks jump, and the rise in yields calms a bit (mostly on the short end of the curve).

“In considering the outlook for monetary policy, several participants commented that further downward adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate would likely be appropriate if inflation were to decline in line with their expectations. Some participants commented that it would likely be appropriate to hold the policy rate steady for some time as the Committee carefully assesses incoming data, and a number of these participants judged that additional policy easing may not be warranted until there was clear indication that the progress of disinflation was firmly back on track.” - January 2025 FOMC Minutes

So if we isolate the moves that occurred before 10 am EST, we clearly see that the combined macro data this morning will have shifted markets towards a more hawkish view on rates. This is especially the case since the FOMC minutes (released two days ago) revealed that several FOMC members were focused on inflation data to decide if they thought further easing was appropriate in upcoming meetings (see quote above). In this context, an increase in the core PCE inflation rate to the highest in about a year and a half will do a lot of work to push those members towards a pause instead of a cut.

As a result, we are seeing FedWatch pause probabilities for the March 17-18th and April 28-29th FOMC meetings firm up. The Fed deciding not to change rates in March is almost a certainty (96% chance), and the probability of another pause in April is at 82.1%, up from 78.9% yesterday and 70.1% a week ago.

The Bottom Line: Yes, Q4 GDP was much weaker than expected, but this was due to temporary distortions, and underneath those, the economy looked similar to how it was in previous quarters, with growth a tad slower. The focus, however, is back on inflation, and prices were hot to end 2025, suggesting inflation concerns must be part of the broader market outlook for both stocks and rates.

data.title: Warning: This AI-Driven Sell-Off Could Be Just Beginning
data.description:
Written by Jussi Askola

The market is finally recognizing what we have been warning about for close to a year, which is that AI is a threat to a lot of stocks.

Until recently, Wall Street still saw AI as a universal profit booster, cutting costs and increasing margins. That may be true in the short term, but the problem is that it also breaks traditional barriers to entry, likely leading to much greater competition and lower profitability over the long run.

Digital businesses are the most extreme example of that.

What used to require a team of developers, a few million dollars, and many months to build can now be reproduced with much lower resources.

The barriers to entry have eroded, competition is already surging, and I think that this will inevitably hurt a lot of well-established companies over the long run.

This does not mean that they will collapse overnight, especially if switching costs are steep, but it will likely lead to greater pricing pressure, lower retention rates, and slower long-term growth.

This realization has had a truly spectacular impact on the stock prices of such digital businesses. Market leaders like Microsoft (MSFT), Salesforce (CRM), Workday (WDAY), Roper Technologies (ROP), and Constellation Software (CNSWF) are down anywhere between 20% and 50%, and that’s in less than one year:

If those big established players are down so much, you can imagine that smaller digital businesses must be down even more. Companies like Monday (MNDY), Duolingo (DUOL), PAR Technologies (PAR), etc., are down closer to 70-80%:

The value destruction has been truly massive, and it happened very quickly as the market recognized this risk.

But Here's The Worst Part:

I fear that this is still just the beginning. The market right now appears to still think that only these digital businesses will suffer from AI breaking barriers to entry and inviting more competition.

I think that this is fundamentally incorrect.

Yes, it is true that digital businesses are often the most heavily impacted, especially in the near term. However, I think that AI will ultimately have a negative impact on most businesses. SaaS businesses were just the first domino to fall, but the impact of AI could go far beyond that, and once the market realizes this, we could be in for a much steeper sell-off.

Just Consider These Examples:

There are lots of companies that specialize in simply providing services to others, ranging from law firms to consultants, accountants, marketing agencies, web designers, brokers, videographers, auditors, and lots of other things. Now, most of those services can be provided at a much lower cost thanks to the help of AI. Expertise is being commoditized, competition is rising, and margins will eventually reflect that.

Education is also changing, as learning with a personalized AI tutor that knows exactly your pace of learning and your knowledge level and teaches you in a gamified way is likely far more effective than sitting in a classroom with one teacher and 50 other students, all trying to learn the same thing, at the same pace, in a boring setting.

Healthcare is also being commoditized, with AI already today often replacing doctors to answer a lot of basic health-related questions, but in the future, robots are expected to be performing surgeries. In fact, there are already such robots that are claimed to be far more precise than humans could ever be. Elon Musk has even called it "pointless" to go to medical school at this point.

Retailers will now also face even greater competition from e-commerce companies as their marketing will become far more powerful and personalized thanks to AI. At the same time, AI also makes it far easier than ever before for anyone to set up a new online shop, leading to greater competition across retail, on- and offline, likely reducing margins over time.

Autonomous driving capabilities could also put a lot of transportation-related companies out of business. They have the potential to massively reduce the cost of transportation as well as the number of accidents. How will this impact taxis, logistics companies, car repair shops, insurance companies, parking operators, and much more? Will it even make sense anymore to own a personal car if you can almost instantly order a self-driving vehicle to pick you up at a very low cost that’s competitive with the cost of owning a personal car? This could then collapse the car industry.

And this is just the tip of the iceberg.

There are lots of other examples of businesses that will suffer over time from the disruption caused by the AI revolution.

My point is that SaaS companies were likely just the first domino to fall. They are the easiest and fastest to disrupt by AI because they are purely digital businesses.

But the value destruction could go far beyond that. I expect it to hurt most businesses over the long run as it breaks traditional moats and makes it easier than ever before for anyone to start a new small business in almost any field.

What Is The Solution Then To Protect Your Portfolio?

You want to invest in AI-proof asset classes that won’t be disrupted.

Most often, this means owning real asset-based businesses such as MLPs (AMLP), REITs (VNQ), Utilities (XLU), Miners (GDX), and other listed infrastructure or commodity companies.

Take the example of Energy Transfer (ET), which controls some of the biggest energy pipelines across North America. This is not a business that AI can disrupt. The infrastructure is absolutely essential to our society, and its business is largely insulated from commodity price fluctuations.

Another good example is timberland REIT Rayonier (RYN). It owns 4.2 million acres of timberland, a scarce resource that will remain needed, regardless of what happens to AI next.

A last example: BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA; BSRTF) owns affordable apartment communities in rapidly growing suburbs of Dallas, Houston, and Austin with an average rent of $1,500 per month. AI will never remove the need to have a roof over your head, and these properties are limited in supply by their locations, building permits, financing availability, and construction cost.

And here comes the most interesting part: despite being, in my opinion, some of the most attractive assets to own in an environment in which AI is disrupting most businesses, these companies are often trading at historically low valuations.

We predict that as more investors become concerned about the potential value destruction caused by AI, we will see a lot more capital flow to these AI-proof asset classes, likely pushing their valuations to much higher levels.

At High Yield Investor, we have already positioned our market-beating portfolio to not just reduce risks but also target higher returns over the coming years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Time To Take Profits On Gilead Sciences (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD), my favorite in the HIV drugs market, have risen 23.3% since my previous article, "Gilead Sciences: From All-Time Highs To Higher Highs."

And, of course, the primary reason behind this boom is the strong Q4 numbers.

So, its revenue grew 4.8% year-over-year to $7.93 billion, beating Wall Street analysts' expectations by $246.6 million. Meanwhile, its non-GAAP EPS also came in well above the consensus forecast, as in the past eight quarters, but this time by 3 cents.

And I ask myself, which of GILD's drugs was the key to the growth of these two metrics?

Of its HIV franchise, Biktarvy, Yeztugo, and Descovy again showed strong performances, and from the Liver portfolio, only Livdelzi.

So, sales of Biktarvy, Gilead's flagship HIV therapy, reached $3.97 billion in Q4, up 5.1% YoY and 7.7% QoQ.

And since I'll talk about Yeztugo and Descovy a little later, I think it's important to touch on GILD's oncology portfolio now, specifically its antibody-drug conjugate called Trodelvy.

On January 21, it announced that the prestigious journal NEJM had published the results of the Phase 3 ASCENT-04 study [NCT05382286].

In it, Merck's Keytruda [pembrolizumab] (MRK) plus Trodelvy [sacituzumab govitecan] reduced the risk of death or disease progression by 35% in patients with metastatic TNBC compared to the Keytruda-chemotherapy group.

This combo also demonstrated an acceptable safety profile, with an mPFS [median progression-free survival] of 11.2 months, 3.4 months higher than in the comparator arm.

Thus, I can draw the following interim conclusion, namely, Keytruda+Trodelvy could become the "gold standard" as first-line treatment for metastatic triple-negative breast cancer if approved by the FDA.

By the way, CMO Dietmar Berger briefly commented on GILD’s Q4 earnings call regarding when a decision on sBLA should be expected.

We expect FDA decisions for Trodelvy in first-line metastatic TNBC patients who are not candidates for PD-1 inhibitors and for Trodelvy plus pembrolizumab in first-line PD-L1 positive metastatic TNBC in the second half of 2026.

Ahead of the FDA decisions, the NCCN updated their breast cancer guidelines to reflect the practice-changing nature of these results, reinforcing our confidence in Trodelvy's clinical profile.

So, in this article, I will answer whether, according to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, Gilead still has an attractive risk/reward ratio or whether it's time to downgrade its rating from Buy to Hold or Sell.

Yeztugo and Descovy are Gilead's 'lifeline'

Gilead has a rich portfolio of HIV drugs, but the U.S. exclusivity of some of them has already expired, for example, as in the case of Truvada and Atripla.

And in my view, to at least maintain share in this therapeutics market, it is actively switching patients from these older antiretrovirals to Descovy and Yeztugo.

As I mentioned earlier, Yeztugo [lenacapavir] is an inhibitor of HIV-1 capsid function.

Unlike Descovy and Odefsey, whose mechanism is based on blocking only one step of the HIV life cycle by inhibiting an enzyme known as "reverse transcriptase," lenacapavir targets the function of the capsid protein p24.

As a result, it prevents 3 main stages of the HIV replication cycle, including the transfer of viral cDNA into the nucleus, proper capsid assembly, and disruption of the correct formation of the HIV capsid core.

Of course, such a mechanism of action gives it huge advantages.

In my view, the key ones are that Yeztugo shows potency against a variety of HIV-1 variants and can be combined with other agents, such as bictegravir or teropavimab/zinlirvimab.

By the way, it is rapidly capturing the U.S. HIV drugs market due to its twice-yearly dosing schedule and the results of the Phase 3 PURPOSE 1 study [NCT04994509], in which it demonstrated a 100% prevention rate of HIV-1 infections.

And more importantly for me, the promising clinical data on Yeztugo have translated into sales successes for Gilead.

So, its sales rose by 146.2% quarter-on-quarter to $96 million for the 3 months ended December 31, 2025, beating even my 'best case' scenario by $4 million.

Meanwhile, Descovy generated $819 million in net revenues for Gilead, a 33% increase year-on-year.

But these are just numbers. On the Q4 earnings call, CCO Johanna Mercier sounded quite enthusiastic about Descovy/Yeztugo's prospects in 2026.

Coverage for Yeztugo continues to grow, and I'm thrilled to share that we have achieved our goal of 90% coverage well ahead of our 1-year target. This includes all major payers.

Additionally, approximately 90% of covered individuals can access Yeztugo with $0 co-pay.

We continue to work on an account-by-account basis to support pull-through as quickly as possible. While we have more to do, we are making great progress here as we support clinicians and their offices navigate the new logistics associated with a twice-yearly injectable regimen.

Given our expectations for a steady, durable and long-term build in sales, we expect full year 2026 Yeztugo revenue of approximately $800 million compared to $150 million in 2025, highlighting that Yeztugo is well on its way to achieving blockbuster status.

We continue to offer the most compelling HIV prevention portfolio available, including the 6 monthly Yeztugo injectable with its transformative potential on the HIV epidemic, in addition to DESCOVY for PrEP, the current market-leading branded oral. Our goal this year is to continue to drive rapid adoption of HIV prevention, and we expect both brands to demonstrate robust growth in 2026.

Livdelzi is rapidly taking over the PBC therapeutics market

It's becoming clear to me, as well as Mr. Market, that Gilead hit the jackpot with its acquisition of CymaBay Therapeutics two years ago.

Sales of its flagship, Livdelzi [seladelpar], approved for the treatment of primary biliary cholangitis, reached $150 million in Q4, up five-fold year-on-year, despite the competition from Iqirvo's Ipsen/Genfit (IPSEY) (GNFTY).

And by the way, Dr. Berger made some important remarks regarding the timing of when to expect updated results from the Phase 3 IDEAL study [NCT06060665].

Currently, and based on the results of the Phase 3 RESPONSE trial [NCT04620733], I believe Livdelzi will demonstrate efficacy in treating PBC patients who have an incomplete response to the 'SoC' called ursodeoxycholic acid.

We remain committed to further evaluating Livdelzi to potentially improve the standard of care for more patients with PBC. At the liver meeting in November, we presented late-breaking real-world data showing that Livdelzi is an effective and well-tolerated alternative for PBC patients switching from obeticholic acid.

Later this year, we expect to provide an update from our Phase III IDEAL study, evaluating Livdelzi in PBC patients with ALP levels between 1 and 1.67x the upper limit of normal, patients typically excluded from Phase III studies.

If positive, these data could support the expansion of Livdelzi to incomplete responders to UDCA and potentially enable even more second-line PBC patients to achieve better biochemical and symptomatic control of their PBC.

Risks

I highlight five key risks due to which, in my estimation, Gilead no longer has an attractive risk/reward profile.

One of these is the 8.2% and 5.6% year-on-year decline in sales of Tecartus and Yescarta, respectively.

Takeaway

From 2024 to the end of 2025, Gilead Sciences made tremendous progress in developing its HIV and liver portfolios.

And if I look at the bigger picture, I see that this success is already priced in GILD stock, which, by the way, has risen 136.4% since my first article about it.

Now it is trading just below the +3σ band formed from the VWAP, $107.6.

And given Gilead's non-GAAP P/E ratio [FWD] of 17.5x, which is 36.6% higher than its five-year average, in my opinion, these are two strong signals that this antiviral giant is overbought.

So, my final nine words are that I am downgrading Gilead's rating from Buy to Sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AeroVironment: Shockingly Uninteresting
data.description:
AeroVironment, Inc. (AVAV) is a drone specialist, but the company doesn't have the growth metrics warranting the prior excitement in the stock. While the U.S. government and allies plan to ramp up spending on drones and related technology, the market seems focused on expanding industry suppliers for a more robust industry to meet global threats. My investment thesis is more Neutral on AeroVironment, even with the stock recently down $150 from the 52-week highs.

Meh Forecasts

AeroVironment is involved in multiple promising sectors like autonomous systems, aka drones, space, and cyber. The problem is that the defense technology company only reported 9% pro forma growth in the last reported quarter.

The business sits in the middle of the growing demand for autonomous drones and counter-drone systems, with the Department of War promoting a huge boost in spending, but AeroVironment isn't seeing the numbers in current projections.

The company is only growing in the autonomous systems segment, with revenues of just $302 million, only growing 15.7% YoY. The forecast for FY26 is for revenue to grow in the mid-teen range to reach $1.9-$2.0 billion.

AeroVironment has seen the booking levels grow substantially, offering some potential to capitalize on growing global demand for uncrewed aircraft and counter-drone systems. The company reported FQ2 2026 bookings jumped to $1.4 billion with a contracted award ceiling of $3.5 billion.

The military funding system is complex, with AeroVironment reporting a funded backlog of just $1.1 billion with an unfunded backlog of $3.0 billion. The demand appears to exist for uncrewed aircraft systems (UAS) and counter-UAS (CUAS), but most of the projects aren't even funded yet.

On the FQ2 2026 earnings call, the CEO sets forth a much bigger opportunity than presented by the actual financial projections as follows:

We recognize there is a generational shift in the U.S. Department of Defense's procurement strategy and product needs. Our offerings are designed to meet war fighter requirements, and our strategy is fully aligned with these new practices. We're executing on manufacturing expansion and are confident that we can meet increased demand.

Fair Price

AeroVironment forecasts FY26 revenues of up to $2 billion, yet the company acquired BlueHalo back at the end of 2024 with forecasted revenue of $900 million. The forecast is for growth in the 17% range in 2027 to reach sales of $2.33 billion.

The stock appears fully priced for the upside potential ahead with revenue growth targets in the 15% range through 2030. At an 18.5% EBITDA margin in 2030, up from a 15.5% target for FY26, as forecast by UBS, amounts to a target of ~$650 million.

AeroVironment already has a market cap of $13 billion, with forecasted growth nowhere as appealing as one might expect from the exciting opportunities in the drone segment. The stock trades at nearly 60x the FY27 (Apr.) EPS targets of $4.59 and roughly 20x the UBS EBITDA target for 2030.

The upside potential comes from AeroVironment producing far higher revenues than estimates. The company has already boosted the capacity for the Switchblade product to $2 billion worth of annual sales at the Salt Lake City facility alone with a 100,000 square foot facility, an amount equal to the FY26 revenue target alone.

The risk to the investment story is that the U.S. government and allies don't come through with forecasted plans to increase spending on drones and counter-drone products. The bigger risk is that new entrants into the space outshine AeroVironment and take enough market share to suppress the actual growth rates below current projections of 15% growth.

Due to the BlueHalo acquisition, AeroVironment now has $727 million in long-term debt offsetting the cash balance of $669 million. The company still has a strong balance sheet considering the profitable position, but the scenario isn't as positive now.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that AeroVironment plays in a promising sector, but the company just doesn't have the actual projected growth rates to warrant the current stock price, much less a rally from here. The stock appears set to make another run at $400, where investors should use the opportunity to cash out absent any major developments to alter the financial outlook.

data.title: CVR Energy: From Rising Tiger To Falling Knife
data.description:
When I wrote my latest (rare) Sell rating on a stock on Seeking Alpha, it was just last year when I discussed CVR Energy, Inc. (CVI). I have now been writing for Seeking Alpha for nearly 10 years after I initially began writing about my No Guts, No Glory portfolio back in 2016. In those days I wrote more about undercovered and under-the-radar growth stocks, or occasionally stocks that paid a dividend and might be considered dividend growth stocks, in addition to some income funds and dividend-paying stocks like BDCs and REITs.

Because of my history of writing for SA, the editors sometimes ask me to cover a stock that does not otherwise get a lot of coverage. That was the case almost exactly one year ago when I was asked to review CVI. At that time, I believed that uncertainty regarding renewable energy and regulatory issues would be a harbinger of bad times ahead for the company and the stock, which I rated Sell.

With the new Trump 2.0 administration’s energy policies and plans to roll back some of the IRA legislation passed during the Biden administration, the landscape for renewable energy regulation is changing. This has created consternation and frustration on the part of companies like CVR Energy that have made substantial investments in renewable energy infrastructure over the past several years. Until and unless they can find a buyer for the renewable segment, I believe that CVI stock will continue to underperform and I maintain my Sell rating.

In the time since that article was published a year ago, CVI stock rose to a 52-week high of $41.67 at the end of October and then dropped to a closing price of $21.59 on 2/19/26.

Lo and behold, those rollbacks have occurred and have significantly added to the uncertainty and consternation from companies like CVR Energy, which just reported Q4 2025 results. Those results reflect the negative consequences of events over the past twelve months, as well as the murky outlook for 2026, which I will discuss in more detail below. Because the company has delivered such dismal results despite a recent recovery in the overall energy sector, I maintain my Sell rating on CVI stock, which is consistent with the Wall Street analysts who follow the stock.

Ironically, famed investor Carl Icahn, who owns about 10% of outstanding CVI stock, made substantial insider purchases of nearly 1.5 million shares in the first quarter of 2025. Six months later, IBD was celebrating when its “stock of the day” and Icahn’s “favorite” was breaking out and reaching new highs of above $32.

CVR Energy is now up more than 110% since bottoming in April, part of a 70% advance in the 2025 stock market. Meanwhile, more broadly, oil refiners have also rallied recently in this year's stock market. The 27 stocks in the IBD-tracked Oil&Gas-Refining/Manufacturing industry group have collectively jumped around 20% this year.

It appeared that my Sell call in February was a bad one, and someone even commented on that article in August, six months after my article was published, that I had made a bad call. In my February 2025 article, I had noted that CVI deserved a Sell rating starting in 2024 due to a failed $8B takeover bid for Citgo followed by a suspension in the dividend. I also made mention of the CVI ownership interest in another Carl Icahn-owned company, CVR Partners (UAN). At that time (and still today), CVR Energy owned 37% of UAN, and the stock price of UAN had negatively impacted CVI.

On February 18, 2026, CVR Partners reported Q4 and full-year 2025 results with a net loss of $0.97 per common unit in Q4. Revenue during the quarter was down -6% YOY. So, not really great results for UAN stock, either, although Q1 is looking optimistic with rising fertilizer prices as we approach the spring growing season. This offers CVI and UAN bulls some little green shoots of optimism.

What About Peers?

Perhaps you are thinking that lower crude oil prices (CL1:COM) and tighter crack spreads in Q4 had something to do with the poor performance of CVI. For those who are unfamiliar, crack spreads essentially drive profitability for refiners. As explained by EIA, the drivers of gasoline prices include a number of factors that include the crack spread.

Gasoline is the most widely used petroleum product in the United States. Although gasoline is traded globally and priced at various locations around the world, all these prices tend to move together in the long run because gasoline can be transported between markets, thereby linking their supply and prices. Differences in absolute price levels as well as short-term price changes across the world reflect varying gasoline specifications, refinery maintenance schedules, unplanned refinery outages, transportation constraints, peak consumption seasons, as well as regional inventory levels.

However, if that were the case, then other peers in the refining industry group would also have seen their performance suffer. In fact, over the past six months, CVI delivered a negative 20% return, while peers have delivered from 20% to over 40% returns in the same time period.

Review of Q4 2025 Results

In his summary of full-year and fourth quarter results during the earnings call, newly appointed CEO Mark Pytosh had this to say:

For the full year 2025, we reported consolidated net income of $90 million and EBITDA of $591 million. At the segment level, we generated EBITDA of $411 million in the Petroleum segment, $211 million in the Fertilizer segment and a loss of $22 million in the Renewable segment.

For the fourth quarter, consolidated net loss was $116 million and EBITDA was $51 million. Our fourth quarter results were impacted by the accelerated depreciation associated with the reversion of the renewable deal unit at Wynnewood back to hydrocarbon processing along with extended downtime at the Coffeyville fertilizer facility due to 3 weeks of start-up issues at the third-party air separation plant. We continue to believe the refining and fertilizer market fundamentals look constructive for the next several years, which I will discuss further in my closing remarks.

Before I continue with this analysis, it struck me that the flip-flop between renewable and traditional hydrocarbon processing at the Wynnewood facility is likely one big contributor to the poor performance in the second half of 2025. During the Q3 2025 earnings call, former CEO David Lamp (who retired at the end of 2025) discussed the frustration and conflagration around the renewable energy credits that were driving decisions to invest in plant upgrades to Wynnewood.

We do not currently have any additional turnarounds planned in the refining section for the duration of '25 or '26, and we currently expect the next planned turnaround to be at the Wynnewood refinery in 2027.

Average RIN prices for the third quarter were approximately $6.33 a barrel, nearly 25% of the Group 3 2-1-1 crack. Regarding RFS, after years of fighting for the rights of the Wynnewood refinery -- the rights that the Wynnewood Refinery Company is entitled to, EPA in August finally ruled on a backlog of 175 outstanding SRE petitions covering the past compliance period that have been pending before it for years.

In addition to affirming its prior grants of the Wynnewood Refining Company's 2017 and 2018 petitions, EPA granted full waivers for 2019 and 2021 and fifty percent waivers for 2020 and 2022 to 2024. Based on these decisions, we were able to reduce our outstanding RFS obligation on our balance sheet by over 80%. While we continue to believe Wynnewood refinery deserves 100% waivers for every year, we are pleased to have these lingering issues resolved and a large obligation on our balance sheet significantly reduced.

Of course, I cannot blame former CEO Lamp for expressing frustration with the changing rules of the RFS and the slow response by the EPA to make decisions regarding SRE petitions.

Understanding how the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) began, how much the program has changed, and why it even exists in the first place can be challenging. The RFS didn’t simply appear fully formed. It has been reshaped repeatedly by Congress, the courts, market forces, and each presidential administration. Taking a step back to understand the evolution helps explain many of the complexities we navigate today.

As recently as September 2025, a new supplemental proposed rule by the EPA went into effect that changed refinery exemptions, as explained in this posting on Congress.gov.

EPA is issuing "set rules" in accordance with what the agency refers to as the "set authority," the authority for the agency to set applicable volumes for years not specified in the statutory tables. The Set 1 Rule covers the applicable volume requirements and percentage standards for 2023 through 2025, along with regulatory changes. In June 2025, EPA released a notice of proposed rulemaking for the set rule that covers the applicable volume requirements and percentage standards for 2026 and 2027. EPA also proposes to reduce the number of compliance credits (i.e., RINs) generated for imported renewable fuel and renewable fuel produced from foreign feedstocks; remove renewable electricity as a qualifying renewable fuel from the program; partially waive the 2025 cellulosic biofuel volume requirement; update existing renewable fuel pathways and add new renewable fuel pathways; clarify and update compliance reporting requirements; and more.

In September 2025, EPA issued a supplemental proposed rule, partly in response to EPA's August 2025 decision on small refinery exemptions (SREs) for the 2016-2024 compliance years, and partly in response to comments EPA received for the 2026 and 2027 proposed rule. The supplemental proposed rule seeks comments on potential changes to the proposed 2026 and 2027 standards to account for the allocation of exempted fuel due to the granting of SREs.

Those SRE rule changes most likely led to the company’s decision to retrofit the Wynnewood refinery back to hydrocarbon processing, which negatively impacted Q4 results and quarterly cash flow, as explained by current President and CEO Mark Pytosh on the earnings call. It also suggests a planned transition away from renewables and a renewed focus on the Petroleum segment.

"At the segment level, we generated EBITDA of $411 million in the Petroleum segment, $211 million in the Fertilizer segment and a loss of $22 million in the Renewable segment." He noted that fourth quarter results were impacted by accelerated depreciation from the reversion of the Wynnewood renewable diesel unit and extended downtime at the Coffeyville fertilizer facility.

In fact, the company hired a new Chief Commercial Officer, Travis Capps, whom CEO Pytosh introduced on the earnings call to help the company refocus on optimizing the refining portfolio.

Meanwhile, the dividend remains suspended, and there is no indication that the company will resume paying a dividend again any time soon. When asked about the resumption of a dividend during the Q&A part of the earnings call, the company’s response was that the primary focus now is on deleveraging and improving free cash flows before a dividend can resume.

As we've said in our prepared remarks, cash -- free cash flow, minimum cash balances and progress deleveraging have been our priorities. We don't believe that we have to be back to our base $1 billion target before a dividend can return, and we've obviously made a lot of progress on the deleveraging.

… we want a dividend that's going to be sustainable in any part of the cycle. So we want to be able to do that and not yoyo the dividend. And so we -- one of our major goals is to bring the dividend back. We understand that the shareholders would like us to be paying a dividend. But we want also something that's sustainable.

Summary: Don’t Try To Catch This Falling Knife

Some investors might see this latest price drop in CVI stock as a buying opportunity, with the company in the midst of a turnaround. With a new CEO and new CCO on board along with favorable macro conditions for the refining industry, CVR Energy just might be in a position to improve their financial position enough to grow the business and reverse the trend of declining revenues and negative earnings growth.

According to the SA Quant ratings, which rate CVI a Strong Sell, the Revisions trend of revenue projections continues to decline in 2026 with negative YOY revenue growth until 2027. Unless or until the company can turn the situation around, I would avoid the stock.

data.title: Anheuser-Busch InBev: Successful Premiumization And Diversification Efforts - Overbought Rally Warrants Caution
data.description:
I previously covered Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD) (OTCPK:BUDFF) in September 2025, discussing how its meltdown had already triggered an improved margin of safety, attributed to the stock's prior overbought levels and the growth headwinds in Brazil/China.

Despite this, given the growth tailwinds from the no-alcohol beer segment/premiumization/in-home channel/intensified digital sales efforts, the richer profit margin, and the cheap valuations, I had believed that the stock offered a high double-digit upside potential, resulting in my Buy rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the BUD stock, albeit after a slight moderation, attributed to their well-diversified beverage portfolio, the expanding profitability despite the declining volumes, the rich cash flows delivering robust shareholder returns/deleveraging, and the still compelling valuations despite the recent double-digit rally.

My caution about the stock price is attributed to the notably overbought technical indicators across the elevated RSIs/trading volumes and the seemingly exhausted upward momentum at the recent high of $80s, with investors well advised to wait for a moderation to the 50-day moving averages for an improved margin of safety.

BUD Delivers Successful Premiumization & Diversification Strategies

Since my last Buy rating, BUD has charted an outsized total return of +32% compared to the wider market at +5%, with a similar rally also observed in its beverage peers in varying degrees.

Much of their tailwinds are attributed to the ongoing market rotation to value/dividend-oriented stocks after the notably cooling AI-related sentiments since November 2025, with a similar trend also observed in the REIT, telecom, tobacco, and energy sectors, amongst others.

This is significantly aided by BUD's robust performance in the recent FQ4'25 earnings call with revenues of $15.55B (+4.8% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 35.1% (-0.2 points YoY/+1.5 from FY2023 levels of 33.6%), and EPS of $0.95 (+7.9% YoY) despite the moderating volumes by -1.5% YoY.

This implies their notably improved pricing/brand power as observed in the higher revenue per hl by +4% YoY, despite the tougher comparison from a year ago at +5.5% YoY.

Much of their tailwinds are attributed to BUD's premiumization strategy working as intended, significantly aided by the increasingly efficient sales/marketing spend of $7.4B (+2.7% YoY), comprising 12.4% of its FY2025 revenues (-1.6 from FY2019 levels of 14%).

These numbers are impressive indeed, despite the notably deteriorating volumes sold in the APAC region at -6.3% YoY in FY2025, despite the already underwhelming performance at -9% YoY in FY2024, along with those observed in the South American region at -3.5% YoY/-1% YoY, respectively, attributed to the changing consumer consumption trends and inflationary pressures, respectively.

If anything, BUD's push into the No-Alcohol market has driven the segment's robust revenue growth by +34% YoY in FY2025 and the Beyond Beer segment by +23% YoY, with further growth likely as the No-Alcohol market is expected to grow at an accelerated pace of 3x the overall beer category through FY2029 and the Beyond Beer market at 2x.

This is especially since the management has guided the notably more profitable No-Alcohol segment at +20% on a gross profit per hl basis versus beer in the US, with Beyond Beer boasting a richer premium of up to +30%, lending credibility to the management's aggressive push into these new innovation markets.

These growth efforts are likely to be bolstered by BUD's durable and likely cash-flow-rich legacy Megabrands revenue growth by +4.1% YoY in FY2025, since the segment comprises an expanding ratio of its overall FY2025 revenues at 57% (+3 points YoY/+7 from FY2021 levels of 50%).

This is on top of the management's digital approach through:

  • the BEES Marketplace, a business-to-business e-commerce platform, with the "multiproduct ecosystem with tailored solutions spanning from ordering to delivery—all in one connected platform" already driving growing selling trends and the higher BEES GMV at $53B (+8.1% YoY), along with
  • the D2C business is also delivering excellent active consumer growth to 12.3M (+11% YoY) and growing revenues to $553M (+8% YoY),

... with it highlighting their diversified strategy across drink portfolios and distribution channels to drive profitable growth trends.

While past performance may not be indicative of future performance, BUD's adj EBITDA growth to $21.22B in FY2025 (+4.9% YoY) and the management's adj EBITDA growth guidance at between 4-8% for FY2026 already underscore their confidence in delivering profitable prospects ahead, compared to the historical profile at a 5Y CAGR of +4.1% and 10Y CAGR of +2.3%.

Combined with the accretive impact from the ongoing share repurchases, with -0.9% of their float retired in FY2025, -4.9% since FQ2'21, and the planned $5.6B of share repurchases through FY2027 (approximately ~70M shares or the equivalent of ~3.5% of their float based on current stock prices of $78), it is unsurprising that the consensus forward estimates appear to be extremely promising, with it building upon the FY2025 EPS growth by +5.6% YoY.

This is with BUD expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line EPS growth at a 3Y CAGR of +5.1%/+14.2% through FY2028, compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +4.8%/+14.3% and 10Y CAGR of +3.1%/-3.3%, respectively.

These reasons are also why BUD continues to trade at a relatively compelling FWD EV/EBITDA valuation of 9.88x, compared to the 1Y mean of 9.27x and the 5Y mean of 9.98x, while still moderated compared to the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 13.28x.

A similar conclusion may also be derived based on my estimated FY2028 EV/EBITDA valuations of 9.02x, arising from the management's medium-term adj EBITDA growth guidance at a CAGR of +6% at the midpoint, the estimated FY2028 adj EBITDA of ~$25.2B, and the Enterprise Value of $227.50B by the time of writing.

When comparing BUD's current FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 9.88x and the management's medium-term adj EBITDA growth guidance at between 4-8% against some of its brewery/beverage/energy drinks peers, including:

  • Constellation Brands (STZ) at 11.33x/the consensus 3Y adj EBITDA growth estimates at -2.5%,
  • The Boston Beer Company (SAM) at 9.51x/+3.3%,
  • Molson Coors Beverage (TAP) at 7.06x/-1.6%,
  • PepsiCo (PEP) at 13.74x/+5.7%,
  • Coca-Cola Company (KO) at 21.40x/+6.7%,
  • Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) at 11.91x/+4.3%,
  • Monster Beverage (MNST) at 30.10x/+10.6%, and
  • Celsius Holding (CELH) at 21.74x/+16.1%, respectively,

It is apparent that BUD remains cheaply valued here, with a potential upward re-rating nearer to the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 13.28x (still cheap against its peer group mean of ~15x) likely to bring forth further upside potential.

This is especially since BUD has used their rich Free Cash Flow generation of $11.33B in FY2025 (in line YoY) to report a healthier balance sheet, as observed in the lower net debts of $60.9B and the net debt to EBITDA ratio of 2.87x in FY2025 compared to the $95.5B/4x reported in FY2019, respectively.

Combined with their moderating capex trends over the past few years, it is apparent that the management has been extremely competent in their:

  • capital allocation for shareholder value creation across dividend/share repurchases,
  • balance sheet deleveraging, and
  • growth initiatives through its mega brands and new innovations through no-alcohol beer/beyond beer.

This is especially since BUD is likely to eventually reach their leverage ratio of ~2x over the next few years, thanks to the rich Free Cash Flow generation and the expanding adj EBITDA profitability - with it lending credence to its compelling recovery investment thesis, compared to the beverage (Wineries & Distilleries) industry median leverage ratio of 4.54x and the beverage (non-alcoholic) industry median of 1.02x.

BUD Remains A Great Buy, Upon A Slight Dip

Thanks to the prior market rotation to the value/dividend-oriented sector, BUD has already broken out of the prior 4-year sideways trading cadence, numerous resistance levels, and the 50/100/200-day moving averages to retest the prior January 2021 top around the $78s by the time of writing.

Based on the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 18.19x (cheap against the peer group mean of ~21x) and the FY2025 EPS performance of $3.73 (+5.6% YoY), it is apparent that the stock has already run away from my fair value estimates of $67.80.

Otherwise, based on the consensus FY2028 EPS estimates of $5.56, there remains an excellent upside potential of +28.1% to my long-term price target of $101.10, despite the +30.5% rally from the December 2025 bottom, thanks to the accretive impact of BUD's richer profit margins and their ongoing share retirement.

While the current forward dividend yield remains a distance away from the 4Y pre-pandemic mean of ~3.5%, readers may want to note that the company already offers a progressive dividend per share growth at a 5Y CAGR of +18.1% (between FY2020 levels of $0.56 and FY2025 sum of $1.29), with further raises likely as they get closer to their leverage target of ~2x.

As a result of the well-diversified beverage portfolio, the expanding profitability despite the declining volumes, the rich cash flows delivering robust shareholder returns/deleveraging, and the still compelling valuations despite the recent rally, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the BUD stock.

This is, albeit with the caveat that interested investors observe BUD's stock prices for a little longer before adding, given the notably overbought technical indicators across the elevated RSIs and trading volumes.

Combined with the seemingly exhausted upward momentum at the recent high of $80s, I am of the opinion that the stock is likely to return part of its recent gains to return to the 50-day moving averages of $68s, with those levels also nearer to my fair value estimates of $67s.

Given the potential -13% downside from current levels, I urge some patience in the meantime.

data.title: Tariff Drama Isn't Over Yet
data.description:
Thesis Summary

The Supreme Court has just struck down President Donald Trump's tariffs on the basis of the fact that they are unconstitutional.

These have buoyed markets, which were up 1% following the news, though they have now given back some gains.

Perhaps, like me, investors have realized that the tariff drama isn’t quite over yet.

The Court didn’t outlaw tariffs outright.

It simply ruled that the way the tariffs were implemented was unconstitutional. This was done using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act.

But the battle isn’t over yet, and the Trump administration has mentioned before that it can look at other ways of carrying out the tariffs.

The tariff tantrum isn't over yet.

What The Court Actually Said

The Supreme Court ruling wasn’t really about whether tariffs are constitutional but rather whether the executive branch has the power to unilaterally levy tariffs like Trump just did.

The administration attempted to use emergency powers to impose broad-based tariffs across trading partners.

But the Supreme Court ruled that emergency economic powers do not equate to blanket authority to tax global trade.

With tariffs invalidated, we will now be left with some messy implications in terms of carrying out refunds.

However, just because these tariffs don’t apply, it doesn’t mean tariffs can’t be reversed.

Let’s highlight a few ways in which tariffs could make a comeback.

Pathway #1: Use Different Statutes

This is the most obvious route.

The administration, or anyone thereafter, could potentially rely on statutes that were not touched by this ruling.

Two major ones remain intact:

  • Trade Expansion Act of 1962 (Section 232).

  • Trade Act of 1974 (Section 301).

The first would allow tariffs on grounds of national security, while the latter would be valid in terms of levying retaliatory tariffs.

It's worth mentioning that both of these avenues have been used before, successfully.

The main difference here would be that instead of a generalized, all-around tariff, the government would have to levy specific tariffs on each country.

Pathway #2: Go Through Congress

With approval from Congress, tariffs could come back.

Is this likely? Probably not very, in my opinion.

In order to get tariffs passed through Congress, you would need some degree of bipartisan support, and that’s quite unlikely in today’s political climate.

On top of that, it's hard to know how things could pan out here, especially considering that we have midterms coming up.

But if something akin to tariffs did pass through Congress, it would probably have to be a narrower, more targeted set of tariffs too.

Pathway #3: Rebrand Tariffs

There are always legal loopholes.

A tariff is a way of making imports more expensive, and there are a few other ways to do this.

  • Border adjustment taxes.

  • Import surcharges framed as industrial policy funding.

  • Environmental or carbon-based trade penalties.

Practically leading to a similar result, but politically and perhaps legally easier to pass through.

However, even if the administration took this route, it could also be short-lived. This is ultimately a legal loophole, not a valid way to change things.

The Market’s Mistake

Markets thrive on clarity, and that’s what the Supreme Court ruling has given us.

However, this may not be as definitive as investors think. Policy risk doesn’t disappear because one legal mechanism failed.

Tariffs could come back, and this renewed drama may very well shake markets again.

The Bigger Picture

Ultimately, I do think it's important to note that tariffs and this trend of de-globalization are likely not limited to this administration.

We’ve started down a path that likely can’t be undone.

It’s early to tell, but I do think globalization as we know it may have peaked. National interest, industrial policy, and securing supply chains have become pressing issues, especially in the age of AI.

So, in the end, I do expect tariffs to come back. Definitely not today, maybe not tomorrow, but very likely someday.

But what does this mean for markets?

For now, this is clearly priced in as good news. Certain sectors will no doubt benefit, but this may also have some longer-term negative implications.

More uncertainty, higher funding needs for the government, and perhaps even more internal instability.

Final Thoughts

The Supreme Court clipped one branch of executive authority, but it did not undo protectionism.

I believe this is a trend that will prevail, and I believe tariffs will make their way back.

The most bearish thing now is the uncertainty that could come with this decision.

data.title: Best Dip Buys With 'Year Of The Fire Horse' Potential
data.description:
Temporary Weakness Presents Dip-Buying Opportunities

As 2026 begins with a market pullback and rotation, investors are confronting a familiar tension. Two of the hardest hit sectors year-to-date are technology and financials, for broadly understandable macro reasons, including lofty valuations, policy uncertainty, and shifting liquidity conditions. Yet beneath the surface weakness lies a compelling narrative. This week began the Year of the Horse in the Chinese zodiac cycle, and more specifically, the Year of the Fire Horse, a rarity that occurs only once every 60 years.

What Is the Year of the Fire Horse?

While Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings don’t account for zodiac symbolism, the 2026 Year of the Fire Horse’s significance captures the spirit of speed, power, and relentless forward motion that aligns with a theme developing in select equities. This theme suggests a time to buy temporarily beaten-down stocks that can continue previous momentum.

The "double fire" element suggests amplified momentum driven by high energy, boldness, and decisive moves, but also heightened by risk. That combination mirrors today’s market, which consists of powerful secular growth themes temporarily interrupted by cyclical headwinds. In what is shaping up to be a stock picker’s year, the recent dip offers opportunity. Select high-momentum U.S. and Chinese stocks appear positioned to gallop ahead as sentiment stabilizes.

How I Chose the Top Dip Buys With High Growth Potential

This group of five stocks is unique in that I didn’t use a single search in the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener to find them. I searched in a few familiar places, such as the preset screen for Best Chinese Stocks and Top Semiconductor Stocks, to find beaten-down names with strong forward growth potential. All five are Quant Strong Buys with stellar Growth Factor Grades.

1. Futu Holdings Limited (FUTU)

  • Market Capitalization: $21.37B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 02/20/2026): 67 out of 673

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 02/20/2026): 3 out of 26

  • Sector: Financials

  • Industry: Investment Banking and Brokerage

Futu Holdings Ltd is a Hong Kong-based digital brokerage and wealth management platform serving retail investors across Asia and increasingly abroad. As capital markets activity stabilizes after the recent dip, Futu’s high Growth Factor grades are supported by strong revenue expansion, rising client assets, and operating leverage tied to trading volumes. Before digging into its growth metrics, FUTU’s valuation is a key to its dip-buying attractiveness.

A quick glance at its Quant Factor report card, you can see that FUTU’s valuation score has improved from a 'D+' to a 'B-' over the past six months. When you click over to the Quant Valuation page, you’ll see a forward P/E that shows a superficial premium to the sector median, but the PEG of 0.45 displays a 56% discount to the sector. The PEG, which factors forward growth potential, segues nicely into FUTU’s Quant Growth metrics. While the top-line revenue growth of nearly 35% is over 4x the sector median, the more telling metrics are forward EBITDA Growth, which at 41% is still more than 3x the sector. The long-term growth (3-5Y CAGR) is also roughly 3x sector peers.

In a Year of the Fire Horse theme defined by momentum and speed, Futu can benefit from renewed risk appetite and cross-border capital flows between mainland China and global markets. Its scalable fintech model positions it to gallop ahead as financial sentiment improves. That same high-energy theme carries into China’s evolving education landscape.

2. TAL Education Group (TAL)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.64B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 02/20/2026): 27 out of 480

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 02/20/2026): 2 out of 26

  • Sector: Consumer Discretionary

  • Industry: Education Services

TAL Education Group provides after school tutoring and smart learning solutions in China, increasingly pivoting toward enrichment, digital tools, and AI-enhanced education services. Despite regulatory resets in prior years, TAL has rebuilt growth momentum with improving margins and strong top-line acceleration, reflected in standout Quant Growth Metrics. Similar to FUTU’s setup, the improving Quant Valuation grade, moving from 'D+' to 'B' in six months, helps support the dip-buying narrative.

Like many stocks I’ve seen outperform sector peers, TAL has an average P/E, but when adjusting for forward growth expectations, the valuation looks much more attractive. For example, TAL’s forward P/E is 16.60, which is only about 1.5% below the sector median, but its forward PEG is 0.36, which offers nearly a 79% discount to the sector. Supporting this PEG are TAL’s Quant Growth metrics.

TAL’s asset-light capital expansion offers operating leverage in 2026. In the Fire Horse spirit, TAL embodies resilience and renewed forward growth drive following adversity. From China and education’s rebound, we shift to the U.S. and high-performance semiconductor connectivity powering the AI buildout.

3. Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO)

  • Market Capitalization: $23.60B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 02/20/2026): 11 out of 536

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 02/20/2026): 3 out of 70

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

Earning a spot in my Alpha Picks portfolio in February 2025, Credo Technology Group designs high speed connectivity solutions for data centers, enabling bandwidth demands associated with AI and cloud infrastructure. While technology stocks have broadly dipped in 2026, Credo’s accelerating revenue growth supports its 'A+' Growth Factor grade. High AI capex spending remains a tailwind, even amid near-term volatility, and Credo picked up a bit more momentum this week, receiving a Buy rating from Goldman Sachs due to CRDO’s edge in data centers. As I structured the other stock analysis in this article, we’ll look at valuation metrics, followed by growth.

When the market is in a broad momentum phase, pullbacks tend to punish growth stocks with high P/Es, and CRDO is no exception, with a lofty 41.84 valuation. Year-to-date, CRDO is down about 10%, and it’s a little over 30% off its all-time high price set in November. But a momentum-driven market generally comes back to the growth leaders, and CRDO is now on a 9% upswing over the past five trading days. This is why I focus less on P/E and more on PEG, which is attractive for CRDO at 0.54, indicating more room to run. This segues nicely into Credo’s Growth Factor metrics. The company’s estimated forward EBITDA growth is giant at over 281%, which is 19x the sector median, while the long-term earnings growth (3-5Y CAGR) is over 77%.

In a "double fire" year symbolizing energy and bold expansion, Credo stands at the center of digital infrastructure momentum. From hardware enabling AI, we turn to the software backbone powering modern applications.

4. MongoDB, Inc. (MDB)

  • Market Capitalization: $28.97B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 02/20/2026): 16 out of 536

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 02/20/2026): 2 out of 23

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Internet Services and Infrastructure
